{"covered_dims": {"R1.2": 0.6, "R1.3": 0.7, "R1.5": 0.5, "R1.6": 0.95, "R1.7": 0.3}}

Revised the draft to eliminate the heavy redundancy (sentences that restated themselves), tighten phrasing, and sharpen the evaluation claims. Substantive content (methods, citations, numbers) is preserved.

---

In this section we present a methodology for cross-validating exoplanet candidate detections that addresses the false-positive contamination problem affecting transit surveys such as Kepler and TESS. Our approach combines vetting signals from multiple independent classifiers to improve overall reliability.

The pipeline ingests light curves from the TESS Science Processing Operations Center (SPOC) pipeline (Jenkins et al. 2016) and applies the Box Least Squares (BLS) periodogram (Kovács et al. 2002) to identify candidate transit signatures. Surviving candidates are then fit with a Mandel & Agol (2002) transit model, parameterized by planet-to-star radius ratio, impact parameter, and quadratic limb-darkening coefficients. Posterior sampling uses emcee (Foreman-Mackey et al. 2013), the affine-invariant ensemble sampler widely adopted in the exoplanet community.

Candidates passing the BLS+MCMC stage are then reviewed by three independent classifier agents: (i) a centroid-shift discriminator following Bryson et al. (2013), (ii) an odd-even transit-depth comparator, and (iii) a Gaussian-process variability test built on the celerite framework (Foreman-Mackey et al. 2017). Each agent emits a reliability score, and the three scores are combined through a weighted consensus rule.

We evaluated the consensus pipeline on the TESS Object of Interest (TOI) catalog, restricted to TOIs with confirmed dispositions in the NASA Exoplanet Archive. The pipeline recovers 92% of confirmed planets and flags 87% of known false positives — rates competitive with prior vetting frameworks such as Robovetter (Thompson et al. 2018) and DAVE (Kostov et al. 2019).

---

Remaining issues the author should address (not fixed in the revision):

1. The opening still asserts a contribution ("combining vetting signals from multiple independent classifiers") without specifying what is novel relative to Robovetter and DAVE, both of which also aggregate vetting tests. A single sentence on the delta (e.g., probabilistic per-agent scoring, GP variability test, or specific agent architecture) would strengthen the framing.
2. The "weighted consensus rule" is mentioned but not defined — readers will want to know how weights are set (fixed, learned, calibrated on a holdout set?) and whether the 92%/87% numbers depend on a tuned threshold.
3. The evaluation has a possible selection-bias concern: restricting to TOIs with *confirmed* dispositions omits ambiguous cases, which are precisely where vetting matters most. Worth acknowledging or addressing.
4. "Competitive with Robovetter and DAVE" is asserted but not quantified. If those frameworks' published recovery/FP rates are available, give the numbers; otherwise soften to "of comparable order."
5. Citation coverage is thin for the false-positive problem framing — consider adding a foundational reference (e.g., Morton & Johnson 2011, or Coughlin et al. 2016 for Kepler completeness/reliability) where the contamination problem is introduced.
6. Verify Bryson et al. 2013 is the intended centroid-shift reference (Bryson et al. 2013 PASP on Kepler centroid analysis is the standard; confirm year/venue).

Uncertainty: recommendations 4–6 depend on the broader paper's scope and on bibliographic details I cannot verify from the excerpt alone; the suggested citations are standard in the subfield but should be checked against the author's reference list.
